This is a new data point that suggests current growth is sustainable and doesn't just represent the company rapidly working its way through its pipeline.

BrainsWay's operating profit totaled $430,000 in the fourth quarter at a 3.8% margin. Adjusted EBITDA was $1.4 million at a 12% margin. While BrainsWay's margins are continuing to move higher, recent gains have been fairly modest, which has likely been driven by an increased focus on growth.

BrainsWay was profitable and cash flow positive throughout 2024 and guidance suggests this situation is likely to continue in 2025. The company has suggested a 3-4% operating margin and an 11-12% adjusted EBITDA margin for the full year, meaning margins will be fairly flat YoY.

BrainsWay had close to $70 million cash at the end of 2024, with the sequential increase largely driven by Valor Equity Partners' investment. I remain puzzled as to why existing investors were diluted and what this capital is needed for though. BrainsWay initially suggested that the additional cash would help it to explore new markets, revenue channels, and commercial partnerships. Even with increased investments in 2025, the company is still likely to be cash flow positive. BrainsWay has also suggested that it is focused on organic growth, seemingly ruling out any acquisitions.

Conclusion

BrainsWay's valuation is still reasonable given that it is a profitable, high-growth medtech company with a strong competitive position. The company's forward EV/EBITDA ratio is only around 20x, and the company has a long growth runway and plenty of room for further margin expansion.

This matters little at the moment though. BrainsWay's share price has been driven by cash flows in recent years, meaning returns are likely to be weaker while margins aren’t rapidly improving. I also believe that a lot of the upside has been taken off the table if the company is not going to return capital to shareholders at a depressed valuation.

Consideration also needs to be given to the fact that BrainsWay’s business has proven sensitive to the business cycle in the past, meaning there is significant downside risk in the event of a recession. BrainsWay's large cash balance means that it is well positioned to weather any downturn, but this won't necessarily stop its stock from declining at least 30-40% from current levels in the event of a recession.

data.title: Mastech Digital Is Valued Like A Turnaround, Now It Needs To Earn It
data.description:
Thesis

Mastech Digital, Inc. (NYSE: NYSE:MHH), a U.S. company that offers IT staffing and digital transformation services, has for quite a while worked in the space where older workforce solutions meet newer technology consulting. Lately, though, it’s been putting more focus, gradually but steadily, on building up its data and analytics capabilities.

I initiated coverage on the company with a “Hold” rating last September, after it had gone through a stretch of underperformance, there was a drop in revenue and a noticeable slowdown in project activity. That said, it did have a solid balance sheet at the time, and there were some early, if cautious, signs that its more profitable areas were starting to pick up again.

Since then, Mastech has shown clear improvement in margins and overall profitability. Even so, it’s still very much in a period of strategic transition under new leadership. There’s no forward guidance to speak of, and progress on growing revenue has been fairly limited. So, while the operational gains are certainly worth noting, my view is that MHH is priced about right for where it stands, a company with real potential, but one that hasn’t yet demonstrated the steady growth, reliable execution, or clear strategy needed to support a stronger rating.

Strong Quarterly Gains

Mastech Digital’s Q4 2024 revenue for the quarter reached $50.75 million, up 10.2% year-over-year, a solid headline number underpinned by a 26.2% jump in Data and Analytics Services revenue and 6.8% growth in IT Staffing. The Data and Analytics segment in particular stood out, climbing to $10.3 million and demonstrating not just year-over-year strength but sequential momentum as well, up 9.4% from Q3.

Gross margins also impressed, reaching a company record of 29% in Q4. Both business segments contributed. Data and Analytics Services posted a 480 basis point expansion to 49.5%, approaching its internal target of 50%, while IT Staffing improved by 350 basis points, aided by an uptick in direct hire activity and lower healthcare costs. The result was a non-GAAP net income of $2.8 million [$0.23 per share], nearly doubling the prior year’s $1.3 million, and a return to GAAP profitability: $0.3 million compared to a loss of $5.4 million in Q4 2023.

Based on my perspective, these figures indicate that the company has momentarily reversed its negative operational patterns. The annual results support the turnaround story as gross margins improved to 27.9% in 2024 from 25.4% in 2023. GAAP EPS shifted from a $0.61 loss to a positive $0.28 figure while non-GAAP EPS increased to $0.71 marking a 61% improvement compared to the previous year.

But that’s just one side of the story.

Demand Weakness Lingers

Despite the margin wins, it’s impossible to ignore the undercurrent of demand-side fragility and execution risk that came through on the earnings call. Case in point, CFO Jack Cronin acknowledged that “order bookings in Q4 2024 totaled $11 million, which was somewhat below our expectations.” He went on to note that project delays, particularly in the Data and Analytics segment, were driven by a “little bit of uncertainty in the marketplace.”

The implication is that while margins are recovering, the top-line growth story is not yet secure. Client hesitancy, longer decision cycles, and an unpredictable macro backdrop continue to weigh on near-term momentum. And it’s not just anecdotal. Q4 revenue missed consensus estimates by $1.21 million, a notable gap for a company with a sub-$120 million market cap.

Compounding this concern is the seasonal headcount reduction in IT Staffing: 63 billable consultants rolled off projects in Q4. While the company framed this as typical year-end behavior, it’s still a material contraction that will likely show up in Q1 2025 revenue softness.

No Strategic Visibility

Equally important is the vacuum in forward guidance. CEO Nirav Patel, just 45 days fresh into his tenure at the time of the call, emphasized that he is still “listening and learning” and taking “a structured approach in defining our long-term strategy.”

In other words, Mastech isn’t ready to articulate a roadmap yet, not for growth, not for acquisitions, and not for product direction.

For investors, I'd say that leaves a dangerous blind spot. So without a clear strategy or measurable targets, the company's improving margins feel less like the beginning of a durable inflection and more like a defensive maneuver in a still-fragile environment.

To be fair, Patel’s comments did strike a confident tone around Mastech’s position in the AI-led transformation space. “I have no doubt that the AI revolution is real,” he said, adding that companies are “rapidly scaling these capabilities to drive competitive advantage.”

At this point, the whole “AI revolution” storyline should be familiar to just about anyone who’s been paying attention to the market over the past few years. It’s starting to sound less like insight and more like something lifted straight from a template.

In reality, the challenge for Mastech, though, will be proving it can capture that opportunity in a scalable, differentiated way. As of now, its bookings shortfall and flat full-year revenue, $198.9 million in both 2023 and 2024, indicate it hasn’t yet figured that part out.

From a capital position, Mastech remains healthy. It closed the year with $27.7 million in cash and no bank debt, offering dry powder for future investment. It also extended its share buyback program through 2026, with 423,000 shares remaining authorized.

But cash is only as valuable as the opportunities a company can effectively deploy it into. And right now, with M&A on pause and strategic initiatives still under development, that liquidity isn’t translating into immediate value creation.

Valuation and Growth Potential

As I see it, Mastech Digital’s valuation is starting to look more like a discounted bet on what might happen, rather than a premium price for what already has.

At 13.72 times blended P/E, the stock does look inexpensive, especially when you consider it drops to 9.81 times based on the 2026 earnings estimate. That sort of multiple usually suggests one of two things: either the market has overlooked something, or it sees a real risk in how the company will execute going forward.

In Mastech’s case, I tend to think it’s the latter.

The lack of near-term growth clarity is what ultimately undermines my investment thesis, especially when juxtaposed against the optimism baked into 2026 earnings forecasts. I think that optimism might be mathematically justifiable on paper, but it’s not yet evident in the company’s operations or strategic direction.

In the last earnings call, Patel acknowledged as much, stating plainly, “I am taking a fairly structured approach in defining this strategy,” while adding that he plans to share specifics “sometime later [in] the second half of this year.” That vagueness isn’t unusual for a new CEO, but for me, it’s problematic for valuation, especially for a stock that depends on near-term inflection to justify even modest rerating.

Margins vs. Cash Flow

One important concern that may have slipped under the radar is the growing gap between Mastech’s margin performance and its actual cash generation. The 29% gross margin in Q4 looks good on the surface, no question there. But at the same time, operating cash flow fell 55% from the year before, and levered free cash flow dropped by 66.6%. That kind of split suggests that the gains in margin aren’t yet showing up in how efficiently the company runs or how well it uses its capital.

On the call, CFO Jack Cronin didn’t sugarcoat this either. While proud of margin improvements, he provided no commentary on capital allocation beyond maintaining current SG&A levels and keeping CapEx minimal, less than $1 million for the year.

That conservative posture speaks volumes. A company truly leaning into growth doesn’t hunker down on spend; it scales selectively. Mastech, in contrast, appears to be playing defense with its cash position despite a clean balance sheet and $27.7 million in liquidity.

Revenue Stuck in Place

The muted topline adds to this narrative of strategic limbo. Despite strength in Data and Analytics Services we covered earlier, which rose 26.2% year-over-year, the overall revenue figure for 2024 was unchanged. That’s not a rounding error, it’s a sign that the business, as currently structured, lacks growth momentum. Cronin pointed to a few project delays and some general market uncertainty as the reasons the company came up short. And while that may very well be the case, it doesn’t do much to reassure me that Mastech is prepared to manage things now that demand is starting to return to a more typical pace.

The issue of customer concentration looms large in this context.

As Nirav Patel himself noted, Mastech needs to transition into “an AI-first organization” with differentiated offerings, but the roadmap for that transition is still vague. In the meantime, the firm remains heavily exposed to a handful of large clients in its staffing segment, a point of vulnerability that could unravel revenue stability further if even one of those relationships is disrupted. And that’s not a hypothetical concern. The Q4 reduction of billable consultants was framed as seasonal, but it highlights how fragile staffing utilization can be in a labor-sensitive model. The ripple effect is real, particularly as it threatens to weigh on Q1 2025 results.

Final Verdict

I’m maintaining my "Hold" rating for MHH. In any valuation framework, the question is always: what are you paying for, and what are you actually getting? With MHH, investors are paying for a company that looks cheap relative to its earnings power two years out, but they’re getting a business that has no forward guidance, no near-term revenue acceleration, and a leadership team still in the midst of discovery mode.

Unless and until we see an articulated strategy tied to measurable KPIs and a return to top-line growth, I can’t endorse the idea that MHH is fundamentally mispriced. The discount is real, but so is the uncertainty. This isn’t a margin story anymore. It’s a trust story, and right now, I'm not buying it.

data.title: Golden Ocean Group: Only Time Will Tell The Effect Of Tariffs, Uncertainty Prevails
data.description:
Introduction

I wanted to look at Golden Ocean Group Limited’s (NASDAQ:GOGL) 2024 performance in more detail and give some comments on the potential uncertainty in 2025 and what is keeping me on the sidelines. The overarching theme of tariff war is prevalent throughout the stock market. The company may see a further slowdown in operations, but given its strong financial position, will weather the storm with relative ease in my opinion.

Briefly on performance

Starting from the top, we can see that in the last quarter of the year, the company experienced a decline in revenue. The decline can be attributed to lower TCE rates and fleet utilization, as well as the decrease in on-hire days and fewer operating days in general. There has been a decline in dry bulk freight rates in the fourth quarter driven by softening demand in select commodity groups such as essential Agri bulks like wheat, corn, and soybeans. Overall, because of the previous quarters, the company achieved a respectable y/y growth of around 10% even with the rather disappointing Q4.

Moving on to the company’s efficiency and profitability, which shows us a similar story. The company was enjoying an improvement across the board for the first three quarters of the year and giving some of it back at the end of the year. This stemmed from the same issues as above, as well as higher drydocking expenses and overall higher operational costs. Drydocking expenses skyrocketed to $34.3m compared to $9.7m in Q3. Overall, though, the company did improve its efficiency and profitability for the year by around 300bps across the board.

Regarding some other important metrics, GOGL’s ROA and ROE have somewhat recovered since the end of 2023; however, they are still quite low and could be improved upon, especially when comparing them to its peers. We can see below that the company is somewhere at the bottom of the comparison, but not too much, which means such returns are sector-wide phenomena. In terms of competitive advantage, we can see many companies are unable to surpass 10% ROTC, which tells me that it may be hard to do in this sector, apart from ZIM, which seems to be an outlier. Overall, the management is probably doing the best it can to utilize the company’s assets and shareholder capital; however, there could be some improvements.

Now, let’s look at the company’s financial situation. GOGL finished the year with around $129m in cash and equivalents, as well as $1.2B in long-term debt. How worrisome is this gap? That depends on the company’s operations and cash flow. There are a couple of metrics I like to look at to assess the risk of debt. Firstly, I like to look at the company’s operations at the end of the year and what they pay out in interest on the debt outstanding. The company’s operating income came in at $314m, while it paid out $108m in interest expenses. This means that its interest coverage ratio is around 3x.

Many analysts believe that anything over 2x is healthy. I like to see at least 5x, but that’s just me being more conservative. Secondly, its operating cash flow was around $365m, which also easily covers its interest expense on the debt outstanding. And thirdly, its debt-to-equity is around 0.62, which indicates a balanced capital structure, and it seems to be below the maritime industry average, which is around 0.76.

The interest rate on the debt outstanding is rather high, so the payments are quite high too. Any further increases in interest rates leave the company rather exposed; however, we may have seen the end of the interest rate hikes for now, and maybe we should expect USD SOFR rates to come down in the long run.

Overall, I don’t see any liquidity risk here. The company generates decent cash flow, and its operations are profitable, which should help it whether any sort of downturn that we may experience going forward.

Comments on the Outlook

Speaking of a potential downturn... The company expects further declines in TCE rates for at least the first quarter of 2025. The projected TCE rates are $15,100 for Capesize vessels and $9,900 for Panamaxes. That is a significant reduction even from Q4 ’24, not to mention Q3 ’24, which most likely led to the company cutting its dividend by half. I don’t think this is going to be fixed in the next couple of quarters due to the uncertainty in the economy overall. Many analysts believe that the trade war will be a net negative for many industries, including dry bulk carriers such as GOGL. This will bring a lot more volatility in TCE rates and demand, which will be very hard to predict going forward, mainly because we do not know what the new Trump Administration has in store.

The imposition of tariffs on China and many other countries could disrupt the well-established trade routes, leading to a shift in cargo flows. The Trump administration is going to announce the new Liberation Day plan at 4 pm ET, which will be reciprocal tariffs, like 25% on Canada and Mexico, as well as tariffs on lumber, copper, pharma, and microchips. All this tariff uncertainty is bringing a lot of volatility to the stock markets, with wild swings from deep in the red to green in a matter of hours.

We still do not know what the end plan is of this administration, but recession fears due to tariffs are rising, and I do believe that there will not be a winner in the end, despite what Trump insists upon. This will be a net-negative, although, I would like to be proven wrong. Below is an overall idea of what the administration already has in effect and what might be still in store going forward.

Grain exports of key dry bulk commodities like corn, and wheat may decline because of retaliatory measures and higher costs due to tariffs is certain. It will be interesting to hear what the management will have to say in the next quarterly conference call, which is scheduled in May, and whether they will address the tariff issue this time around. I would say this will be a huge talking point for analysts on the conference call. Also, I wouldn’t be surprised if we get further updates on the company’s outlook, which most likely will be to the downside.

Closing Comments

There is too much uncertainty surrounding Golden Ocean Group Limited, and given that the next quarter seems to be even worse, I don’t think this is the right time to jump in just yet. Even after such a decline in share price, around 50% from recent highs, it still seems that the bottom is not there yet given the ongoing tariff uncertainty and overall elevated volatility. Trump’s Liberation Day announcement will be just the beginning of another back-and-forth flip-flopping show, which means volatility will remain elevated for a lot longer, which already seems to be going on for well over a month.

I am going to be paying attention over the next few months to gauge the sentiment of the investing community and whether that sentiment turns positive regarding the proposed tariffs. What I will be looking for is that the fear of tariffs was overblown all along, and we can return to an uptrend, however, that remains to be seen.

Nevertheless, the company isn’t going anywhere anytime soon. Positive cashflows and profitable operations should help it weather this uncertainty. I just don’t think it’s the right time to jump in just yet.

data.title: Alexandra Real Estate: More Problems Brewing, Stay Out
data.description:
On our previous coverage of Alexandria Real Estate Equities, Inc. (NYSE:ARE), we were not swayed by the bullish arguments. Despite some interesting valuation metrics, we decided to stay out.

You can argue that the stock is cheap at about 10X FFO. It is. You can argue that it is hard to buy BBB+ rated companies at this multiple. That is true as well. You are also likely to make money from here over the next 10 years. It seems like a mathematical impossibility that your total returns would be negative. But is this a table-pounding buy? We are going to take a pass.

Source: You Just Got Litt Up!

We did not miss much by staying out.

Bulls have been treated to 6 additional "Strong Buys" on Seeking Alpha. We have looked at the recent data and decided that we are actually more concerned today than we were a few months back.

Expanding Our Thesis

We won't repeat the work in the first article, so investors need to read that piece before they get to this. But we are going to be looking at how the data from then, is shaping up today. From everything we have been seeing, the problems are getting worse.

About 42% of the Big Apple's life-sciences stock was vacant in the fourth quarter — more than double the average vacancy rate of 20.5% across 24 other cities.

Source: Biz Journals

Both those numbers look scary. For context, ARE's vacancy for New York and other major locations is shown below.

Boston, which is the biggest hub for ARE and the US as a whole, is experiencing an absolute collapse in occupancies.

“Vacancies continue to rise with each passing quarter. The total availability rate of 26.9%—one of the highest in the nation—is 10 percentage points higher than availability one year ago,” says the Colliers report. “New construction was a major contributor to the increased vacant space, adding pressure to an already softened market.”

Key Takeaways from the Report:

Increased Vacancy: In the fourth quarter of 2024 alone, over one million square feet of space were delivered to the market, much of it remaining empty. This surge in new supply further amplified the already-high vacancy rates.

Negative Net Absorption: For the first time in more than a decade, the Boston life sciences market saw negative net absorption during the fourth quarter of 2024, pulling the annual total into the red. This marks a significant shift, as demand for space has not kept pace with the increasing supply.

Surplus of Available Space: Currently, there are over 15 million square feet of available life sciences space in the Boston area, a concerning figure for landlords and developers.

Source: Boston Real Estate Times (March 12, 2025)

We can see some other markets in the picture below.

We think San Francisco Bay area is the more vulnerable as both its lab vacancy and office space vacancy are close to 30%. We also have massive conversions planned there, further increasing pressure on lab space rents.

We always bet on some level of convergence over time. Yes, ARE's properties are better and the Mega Campus model is more resilient. But lab space is fungible. If there are huge vacant spaces, landlords will genuflect to get the tenants in. This will pressure either rents or occupancy or both on ARE's side. Already, we have seen ARE's guidance for 2025 ending occupancy to be the lowest in two decades. We are predicting this will get worse. While ARE's leases are well spread out, the sheer vacancy levels in its major footholds will force rents and occupancies lower.

One minor positive here is that the new lab space (this does not count conversions) is tapering off in a hurry.

While this is good, it is likely to be a positive beyond 2027 at the earliest. Currently, we don't need a single new square foot built, and well, we are still building.

Demand Side

While the supply side looks bad, the demand side is about to get horrific. The new administration is bringing the hammer down on NIH funding.

The scope of the cuts

The Trump administration announced last month that it is capping indirect costs for NIH research grants at 15%. This change does not affect NIH funding for direct costs, which refer to researchers’ salaries and laboratory supplies. Instead, the policy shift decreases the funds available for indirect expenses like utilities, equipment, security and legal compliance.

Before this shift, universities and other research institutions negotiated an indirect cost rate with the federal government, and it usually was about 50-60% of direct research costs — so this change will hurt. The move is projected to cut billions of dollars from research aimed at developing cures and treatments for diseases like cancer, Alzheimer’s, diabetes, mental health disorders and opioid abuse.

Source: Med City News

Anyone thinking this does not destroy the long case for lab space is hallucinating. The total dollars moving lower means less personnel, less lab size and/or lower rents.

We have not even gone to the persistent targeting of the pharma industry, which has been an ongoing issue since day 1.

This takes one of the most promising technology out of grants. The same technology that yielded breakthrough results against pancreatic cancer, one of the hardest to treat malignancies. This should be absolutely riveting for new US research.

Finally, there is this.

Maybe the actual people leaving will be less. But we are at a state of supply-demand for Lab space, that we really cannot afford anyone leaving. Whether you believe in the actions of the new administration or not, is rather irrelevant. What is relevant is that it will drive ARE out of business.

Verdict

Forget the low FFO multiple. Forget the BBB+ rating. There is no bargain here. There are hurricane sized winds coming your way. We doubt the investment grade rating survives 3 years from now. NAV estimates are trending down, as they should. Last time the mean estimate was near $140/share and we are down to $135 today. Our estimate of realistic liquidation value is under $110/share and moving lower as well. We would stay out and use any rallies to exit. We tend to use "sell" ratings only for stocks we are ready to short-sell. This does not fit that at present, so we are going with a "hold" for now.
